coredata.dc:title: Chemical modification with m-phenylenediamine on the purified carbon nano dots for remarkable enhancement of quantum yields and red-shift of fluorescent emission
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Jian
Fung, Yuen Muk
Wu, Peng
Li, Yuan
Liu, Xiang
Zhang, Kui
coredata.dc:description: Photoluminescence (PL) was the core of carbon nano dots (CDs). However, there were different opinions about origin of the PL. Also, the cause for enhanced PL performance via nitrogen doping was still obscure occasionally. However, the fluorophore certification provided an easier understanding on the PL and the surface group ascertainment facilitated nitrogen doping on CDs via surface modification. Imaginably, if CDs were purified in the first place discussions on the PL would be more convincing. Herein, CDs were obtained by hydrothermal treatment on glucose solution and were purified successively via an extraction of ethyl acetate (represented with gCDs). Nitrogen doping was accomplished successfully via the reaction between amine of m-phenylenediamine and the carboxyl of gCDs (the product was represented with N-gCDs). That realized the notable red-shift of the emission with remarkable enhancement of fluorescence quantum yield. Experiments revealed that aldehyde was the fluorophore and C = C bonds constructed the fluorescent skeleton of gCDs or N-gCDs, which were also supported by ab initio calculations on the fluorescent models for gCDs and N-gCDs. Cellular imaging experiment indicated N-gCDs could penetrate cytomembrance of MCF-7 successfully and stained cytoplasm and nucleus effectively during the cell cultivation, which excelled several nitrogen-doped CDs in this aspect.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Glucose
Carbon nano dots
Purification
Chemical modification
Phenylenediamine
Red shift

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Introduction
Experimental
Chemical reagents
Preparation of gCDs and the purification
Fluorescent responses of gCDs solution on NaClO, KMnO4, aniline and Fehling’s solutions respectively
Chemical modification on gCDs with m-phenylenediamine under reflux heating
Hydrothermal treatment on mixture of gCDs and m-phenylenediamine
Fluorescent responses of N-gCDs solution on NaClO, KMnO4, aniline and Fehling’s solutions respectively
Ab initio calculations on the fluorescent emissions of gCDs and N-gCDs
Cellular viability and fluorescent imaging in the presence of N-gCDs
Characteristic techniques
Results and discussion
Structural analyses on gCDs with IR and XP spectra
Demonstration of the C = C bond in gCDs
Demonstration of the aldehyde group of gCDs
The chemical modification on gCDs with m-phenylenediamine under reflux heating
Hydrothermal treatment on mixture of gCDs and m-phenylenediamine
Effect of varied amounts of m-phenylenediamine on the PL intensities
Fluorescent quenching of N-gCDs by KMnO4, NaClO, aniline and Fehling’s solutions
Analyses on some geometrical parameters of the fluorescent models of gCDs and N-gCDs
Cellular imaging in the present of N-gCDs
Conclusions
CRediT authorship contribution statement
Acknowledgments
Supplementary data
References

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Shenzhen Fundamental Research and Discipline Layout project

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is supported by grants from Shenzhen Fundamental Research Fund ( JCYJ20190813172609404 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Illustration of purifying the CDs by extraction of ethyl acetate and the modification with m-phenylenediamine for preparing the N-doped CDs via reflux heating or hydrothermal treatment. The fluorescent quenching of both the CDs and the N-doped CDs was investigated with trifle KMnO4, NaClO, aniline and Fehling’s solutions respectively for the inference of the fluorophore.

(a) Photograph of the extraction with ethyl acetate; (b) Fluorescence spectra of extractive (blue curve) and raffinate (red curve) (excitation = 320 nm); (c) Fluorescence spectra of gCDs with varied excitations.
(a) IR spectra of gCDs and N-gCDs; (b) Enlarged region between 1800 ∼ 1400 cm−1 of (a) for a better observation; (c) and (d) corresponding to XP spectra of C 1 s of gCDs and N-gCDs respectively.

(a) PL intensity of gCDs varied with gradual addition of KMnO4 solutions; (b) KMnO4 solution is faded completely by the added gCDs (left) and the one without addition of gCDs (right); (c) PL intensity of gCDs is depressed by Fehling’ solution; (d) Photograph of erythrine precipitate produced by mixing 1 mL gCDs with 5 mL Fehling’s solution with a water-bath heating.

(a) UV–vis spectra of gCDs and N-gCDs; (b) Fluorescence spectra of N-gCDs with varied excitation; Inset of (b) shows a bright luminescence of N-gCDs solution under irradiation of a UV light (365 nm); (c) Fluorescence spectra of the products obtained by hydrothermal treatment on mixtures of gCDs solution and varied amount of m-phenylenediamine (excitation wavelength = 400 nm).

Fluorescent responses of N-gCDs upon gradual additions of KMnO4 (63.3 mmol/L), NaClO (1.5 mol/L), aniline (1.0 mol/L) and Fehling’s solutions; Insets of (a) and (c) indicate the fluorescent quenching by KMnO4 and Fehling’s solution respectively.

Photographs of proving C = C bonds and the aldehyde in N-gCDs; (a) 5 μL KMnO4 solution (10 mg mL−1) diluted with 3 mL water; (b) Such KMnO4 solution mixed with 10 μL N-gCDs (5.0 mg mL−1); (c) 10 μL N-gCDs solution (24.2 mg mL−1) diluted with 3 mL water with addition of 4 μL NaClO (1.5 mol/L) then with addition of 5 μL KMnO4 solution (10 mg mL−1); (d) 2 mL N-gCDs solution (4.1 mg mL−1) mixed with 2 mL Fehling’s solution which suffered a heating in water bath; (e) 2 mL N-gCDs solution (10.2 mg mL−1) with addition of 3 mL aniline firstly then with successive addition of 2 mL Fehling’s solution, which suffered a heating in water bath.
Fluorescent models of gCDs and N-gCDs corresponding to Model1 of (a) and Model2 of (b) respectively.
Confocal fluorescence images of MCF-7 stained by N-gCDs solutions (0.05 mg mL−1) under bright field and irradiation of 450 ∼ 490 nm corresponding to (a) and (b) respectively.
Some dihedrals in optimized Model1 and Model2.

head.ce:title: Chemical modification with m-phenylenediamine on the purified carbon nano dots for remarkable enhancement of quantum yields and red-shift of fluorescent emission

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Anhui University of Technology, Maanshan 243002, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Anhui University of Technology, Maanshan 243002, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: The Chinese University of Hong Kong, Shenzhen, Guangdong 518172, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Chinese University of Hong Kong

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: The Chinese University of Hong Kong, Shenzhen, Guangdong 518172, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Photoluminescence (PL) was the core of carbon nano dots (CDs). However, there were different opinions about origin of the PL. Also, the cause for enhanced PL performance via nitrogen doping was still obscure occasionally. However, the fluorophore certification provided an easier understanding on the PL and the surface group ascertainment facilitated nitrogen doping on CDs via surface modification. Imaginably, if CDs were purified in the first place discussions on the PL would be more convincing. Herein, CDs were obtained by hydrothermal treatment on glucose solution and were purified successively via an extraction of ethyl acetate (represented with gCDs). Nitrogen doping was accomplished successfully via the reaction between amine of m-phenylenediamine and the carboxyl of gCDs (the product was represented with N-gCDs). That realized the notable red-shift of the emission with remarkable enhancement of fluorescence quantum yield. Experiments revealed that aldehyde was the fluorophore and C = C bonds constructed the fluorescent skeleton of gCDs or N-gCDs, which were also supported by ab initio calculations on the fluorescent models for gCDs and N-gCDs. Cellular imaging experiment indicated N-gCDs could penetrate cytomembrance of MCF-7 successfully and stained cytoplasm and nucleus effectively during the cell cultivation, which excelled several nitrogen-doped CDs in this aspect.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Introduction
ce:section.ce:para: Carbon nano dots (including carbon quantum dots, abbreviated with CDs herein) attracted extensive interests due to the excellent photoluminescence (PL) performance. As to the PL essence of CDs, there were different opinions. Peng and his coauthour found that the CDs emitted fluorescence when treated with a passivator of 4,7,10-trioxa-1,13-tridecanediamine. The authors attributed the PL to the surface passivation since there were no visible or near-UV chromophores in the passivator, which was not photoluminescent at any visible wavelength [1]. Sun reported that there was no detectable PL from the CDs even they were treated with nitric aicd (2.6 mol/L). However the modification with diamine-terminated oligomeric poly-(ethylene glycol) induced bright and colorful luminescence after excitation [2]. However, more examples demonstrated that the external passivation or modification on CDs was unnecessary for the PL [3,4]. The surface groups were regarded as a passivation layer which played the vital role in the PL [5]. Some of them were the fluorophores that were contributive for the PL. Some were the reactive sites which ensured the reactivity of CDs. The applications of CDs in fluorescent analysis depended mainly on the interaction between analyte and fluorophore. By virtue of this approach, the fluorophore could be inferred by the fluorescent responsibility [6]. Additionally, the rigid fluorescent skeleton was the core of fluorescence of CDs, which should be similar to small fluorescent molecules. Therefore, the fluorophore and the fluorescent skeleton were fundamentality for the PL of CDs. Identification on fluorophore provided a way of understanding PL essence of CDs.

Among the CDs derived from carbohydrates, most of them emitted blue fluorescence with wavelength of ∼ 450 nm and lower quantum yield (QY) that hindering the applications in some circumstances. For instance, CDs made from citric acid [7,8], glucose [5,9,10], histidine [11], polysaccharides [12] or sucrose [4,13] owned less than 450 nm emission with a lower QY. Nitrogen doping was an efficient way of enhancing the QY. For example, urea [14], ethanediamine [15], ethanolamine [16], polyethyleneimine [17,18], diethylenetriamine [19], ethylene imine polymer [20] etc. were the effective dopants that improved the QY terrifically. However, the maximum emission wavelengths of such nitrogen-doped (N-doped) CDs could not beyond 450 nm. It seemed that aliphatic amine could not alter the original blue emission.

Usually, N-doped CDs were prepared by one-pot synthesis, which guaranteed efficiency of the preparation. However, the reaction approaches were difficult to be learned since all the raw materials were put into a reactor at the beginning of the reaction. Hydrothermal treatment or microwave heating accelerated reactions among raw materials. Due to the complication of the reaction carbonization process was also impossible to be acquired clearly even it were usually described schematically [17,21–23]. For instance, N-doped CDs could be prepared by virtue of simultaneous pyrolysis of citric acid and polyethylenimine [18]. Nevertheless, the sole citric acid [24,25] or the sole polyethylenimine [26] could be as a resource of preparing CDs. In this case, it was difficult to learn the mechanism of the carbonization. Which reaction approach was adopted to realize the nitrogen doping? PL essence of such N-doped CDs was therefore hard to be understood.

Surface modification on CDs with amines was an optional way of realizing nitrogen doping. Introduction of aromatic pieces might enlarge conjugated areas that caused red shift of the emission. However, the aromatic pieces should attach to the fluorescent skeleton of CDs directly which formed a coplane between them [27]. For a counter example, the modifiers such as fluorescein, rhodamine B and α-naphthylamine, which containing aromatic pieces, could not tune the emission wavelength of the N-doped CDs [9] since the aromatic pieces could not attach to the fluorescent skeleton directly. An aliphatic ethylene hindered the coplane between the dopant and the fluorescent skeleton. Therefore, surface modification provided an easier understanding on the nitrogen doping of CDs. In this case, ascertainment on the reactive site of CDs was the key for the successive modification.

Imaginably, if CDs were prepared and were purified in the first place the accurate identification on fluorophore and surface functional group of CDs were guaranteed definitely. Thus surface modification on CDs was apt to design according to reactive principle for implementing nitrogen doping. For example, surface carboxyl of CDs should be reactive to primary amino of amine [9]. In this case, the role of the doping was more easily understood since fluorescent measurements on CDs and the modified provided the variation of the emissions. Additionally, the structural changes could be revealed by infrared or X-ray photo-electron energy spectrum. That was contributive for analyzing the fluorescent mechanism of the doped CDs.

Herein, we attempted to purify the luminescent CDs and implemented subsequent chemical modification for realizing red shift of the emission and augment of fluorescent QY. Firstly, the CDs were obtained by pyrolyzing glucose solution in the presence of hydrogen peroxide and successive purification by virtue of a facile extraction of ethyl acetate (represented with gCDs). We had proved the surface aldehyde groups were the fluorophore of several CDs and C = C bonds constructed the fluorescent skeleton [6]. The fluorophore of gCDs was also identified herein with the similar method of fluorescent quenching. The purifying operation ensured accuracy on discussion of the fluorophores of gCDs. Surface carboxylic group should be reactive to amino of m-phenylenediamine, as was illustrated schematically in Fig. 1 . Such an approach provided a clear and accurate understanding on nitrogen doping of gCDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental
Chemical reagents
ce:section.ce:para: D-(+)-glucose monohydrate (98%), hydrogen peroxide (30 wt% in H2O), mannitol (98.0%), ethyl acetate (99%), copper sulfate pentahydrate (99.0%), silver nitrate (≥99.0%), ammonia solution (25–28%), aniline (≥99.5%), o-, m-, p-phenylenediamine and potassium sodium tartrate tetrahydrate (99%) were purchased from Aladdin Company (Shanghai China). Sodium hypochlorite pentahydrate was purchased from TCI Shanghai Company.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of gCDs and the purification
ce:section.ce:para: Weighed 0.5 g glucose and dissolved in 9 mL water firstly. Then 1 mL hydrogen peroxide was added with a lasting agitation for 3 min for a complete mixing. The solution was input into a Teflon vessel with a stainless shell. The hydrothermal treatment was carried out in an electrical drier at 180 °C for 60 min. Then the reactor was cooled under ambient temperature. The produced solution was transferred to a flask and was mixed with 20 mL ethyl acetate. The flask was shaken violently for 20 sec for a completion of extraction. Then it was transferred to a funnel to separate the extractive from the raffinate. Thus gCDs in the extractive were obtained by removing the ethyl acetate completely, which described in Fig. 1. Similar experiment was executed without hydrogen peroxide for demonstrating the enhancement of the fluorescence QY caused by intensive oxidation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent responses of gCDs solution on NaClO, KMnO4, aniline and Fehling’s solutions respectively
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescent intensities of gCDs solution were recorded in the presence of NaClO (0.6 mol L–1, 40, 60, 80, …, 160 μL) or KMnO4 solutions (9.50 mmol L–1, 20, 40, 60 and 80 μL). Additionally, fading of KMnO4 solution caused by gCDs were photographed for demonstrating the C = C bond of gCDs.

Fluorescent response of gCDs upon Fehling’s solution (50 μL) was also checked for inquiring if there were aldehyde groups in gCDs. Also, about 1 mL gCDs solution (5.0 mg mL−1) was mixed with 2 mL Fehling’s solution. Such a mixture was heated with hot water for several hours for checking whether erythrine precipitate was produced at the bottom of the test tube. In addition, the fluorescent response on aniline (1.0 mol L–1 in ethanol, 20, 40, 60, …, 240 μL) was also checked.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chemical modification on gCDs with m-phenylenediamine under reflux heating
ce:section.ce:para: Weighed 25 mg m-phenylenediamine and dissolved in hot water of 8 mL. Such solutions were mixed with 2 mL gCDs solution (5.0 mg mL−1). The mixture (10 mL in total) was heated at 80 °C with a reflux for 4 h. Fluorescence spectra of the produced solutions provided valuable suggestions of evaluating the reaction between gCDs and m-phenylenediamine.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hydrothermal treatment on mixture of gCDs and m-phenylenediamine
ce:section.ce:para: Amounts of 5, 15, 25, 40 and 60 mg m-phenylenediamine were dissolved in 8 mL water respectively. The respective solution was mixed with 2 mL gCDs solution (5.0 mg mL−1) and was suffered from hydrothermal treatment at 180 °C for 2 h. Fluorescence spectra were measured for estimating the effect of varied quantities of m-phenylenediamine on the fluorescent performances. The product corresponding to 25 mg m-phenylenediamine was represented with N-gCDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent responses of N-gCDs solution on NaClO, KMnO4, aniline and Fehling’s solutions respectively
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent responses of N-gCDs for 1, 2 and 3 μL KMnO4 solutions (63.3 mmol L–1) as well as for 0.5, 1 and 1.5 μL NaClO solutions (1.5 mol L–1) were checked for analyzing fluorophore in N-gCDs.

Besides, the fluorescent responses for 0.5 and 1.0 μL Fehling’s solution as well as for 20, 40, 60 and 80 μL aniline (1 mol L–1) respectively were recorded for a similar analysis on fluorophore of N-gCDs.
Photographs of KMnO4 and Fehling’s solutions in the presence of N-gCDs solution were also taken to identify the fluorophore in N-gCDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ab initio calculations on the fluorescent emissions of gCDs and N-gCDs
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the aforementioned analysis on fluorophore of gCDs the fluorescent skeleton of gCDs was established. Phenylenediamine should be bonded directly with this fluorescent skeleton since the involvement of phenylenediamine alerted the fluorescence of gCDs. PBE1PBE method and 3-21G basis set were adopted for geometrical optimizations (in water medium) and fluorescent emission calculations on gCDs and N-gCDs respectively. The theoretical results were compared with actual emissions that certified the inferring on fluorescent skeleton of gCDs or N-gCDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cellular viability and fluorescent imaging in the presence of N-gCDs
ce:section.ce:para: Cellular viability was estimated with MTT method. Cellular imaging was carried out according to the following steps. Firstly, live MCF-7 cells were seeded in a six-well template (3 × 105 cells in each well) which placed in an incubator of 37 °C and 5% CO2 for an overnight. Then they were incubated in 1800 μL culture media mixed with 200 μL N-gCDs solution (5.0 mg mL−1) for 8 h. About 500 μL polysorbate (4%) were added to each well after the culture media were discarded for fixing the cells for 15 min. Finally the polysorbate solutions were dumped followed with addition of 1 mL PBS solution in each well. The fluorescent imaging of the cells with excitation of 450 ∼ 490 nm was recorded by a confocal fluorescent microscopy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characteristic techniques
ce:section.ce:para: X-ray photoelectron energy (XP) spectra of gCDs provided the structural information (BRUKER 400, TMS as an internal standard; KRATOS AMICUS). The variation of infrared (IR) (Nicolet 380, Thermo Nicolet Corporation) and XP spectra of gCDs and N-gCDs provided evidences of the chemical modification on gCDs by m-phenylenediamine. All the fluorescence spectra were collected with Perking Elmer LS45. Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) spectrum of gCDs or N-gCDs was measured for explaining the excitation wavelength of fluorescence spectrum. Fluorescent QY of N-gCDs were calculated by comparing with standard substance of Rhodamine 6G (QY = 95% in aqueous medium) as was depicted in our previous work [28]. The morphology of gCDs or N-gCDs was indicated by image of transmission electron microscopy (Tecnai G2 F20 S-TWIN).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Results and discussion
ce:section.ce:para: CDs derived from glucose via pyrolysis have been described in several reporters [4,5,29]. In these works, the fluorescent performance has been discussed in detail. But they do not refer to the purification of the product. It is possible that glucose is converted incompletely. Additionally, some water-solubility byproducts may be produced during the pyrolysis. In these cases, it may be inaccurate in discussing the fluorescent performance due to the impurity. Although column chromatograph is a well candidate way of purifying CDs it needs complicate operation with a lower efficiency [30].

Fig. 2 (a) indicates that when the pyrolyzed glucose solution is mixed with ethyl acetate with a violent shaking the fluorescent substances are extracted. There is a distinct boundary between extractive and raffinate. The impurities with water-solubility or unconverted glucose are removed completely. Hence ethyl acetate plays a role of purifying the fluorescent substance with easy operation and high efficiency. The ester layer emits a bright blue fluorescence under irradiation of UV-violet light while the water layer is not luminous judged by Fig. 2(a).

The fluorescence spectra corresponding to the ester and water layers also prove the fact, shown in Fig. 2(b). The fluorescent intensity of gCDs (ethyl acetate extractive) is more than double of that of the raffinate (both concentrations are 0.209 mg mL−1). The emission peak of gCDs shifts with varied excitations shown in Fig. 2(c). It reveals the inhomogeneity of the gCDs. This is demonstrated by image of transmission electron microscopy of Fig. S1 of Supporting Information. The fluorescent QY of gCDs is 7.2 % only.

In fact, the phenomenon in Fig. 2(a) still occurs to the pyrolyzed glucose solution in the absence of hydrogen peroxide, which exhibited in Fig. S2 of of Supporting Information. The fluorescent substance can also be extracted by ethyl acetate. However, the fluorescent intensity of the extractive is much weaker than that of the one in Fig. 2(a) (QY = 3.2 %). Obviously, hydrogen peroxide, as a violent oxidant, contributes the increase of the fluorescent QY. These results demonstrate that the fluorescent enhancement should be concerned with the intensive oxidization. This provides a suggestion that fluorescence may be arisen from oxidation, which was put forward in our previous researches [26,31].

The gCDs derived from glucose should be composed of plenty of oxygen-contained functional groups [11,17,30,32] due to the multitude hydroxyls. Carboxyl, carbonyl, ether groups, etc may be generated after hydrothermal treatment due to oxidization or reactivity of hydroxyl. All these groups are hydrophilic. Hence the obtained fluorescent gCDs should be hydrophilic assumably. Unexpectedly, the fluorescent intensity of water layer is much weaker than that of ester layer. It is very interesting that the fluorescent substance derived from hydrophilic glucose becomes hydrophobic. It suggests the huge variation in chemical structure. This evokes us to explore what is the fluorophore that is responsible for the fluorescence of gCDs. The extraction operation ensures the accuracy of successive exploring since inorganic impurity is removed entirely.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural analyses on gCDs with IR and XP spectra
ce:section.ce:para: IR spectrum of gCDs in Fig. 3 (a) exhibits the existence of methyl and methylene in gCDs. The weak peak at 2962 cm−1 is arisen from stretching vibration of C-H in methyl. Peaks at 2921 and 2854 cm−1 are due to the asymmetrical and symmetrical stretching vibrations of C-H in methylene. Such groups favor the solubility of gCDs in ethyl acetate. Hydroxyl group in gCDs is demonstrated by peak at 3433 cm−1. The weak peak at 3014 cm−1 should be arisen form stretching vibration between an unsaturated C and H, which means the elimination reaction during the pyrolysis of glucose solution. In Fig. 3(b), the peak centered at 1624 cm−1 should be arisen from overlapping of C = O (aldehyde or ketone) and C = C bond. C = O bonds may be derived from either glucose itself or the violent oxidization of hydroxyl. A weak peak at 1715 cm−1 should be due to stretching vibration of C = O of carboxyl. This is due to the oxidation of glucose.

XP spectrum of C 1 s in Fig. 3(c) indicates there are sp3 or sp2 hybridized carbons in gCDs due to the peak at 284.8 eV [5,33]. IR analysis and such a datum suggest C = C bonds in gCDs that contributes molecular rigidity and favors the luminescence of gCDs. Binding energy at 286.5 eV is derived from C–O. Peak at 288.0 eV should be due to C = O [33] of aldehyde or ketone group, which may be involved in the π-π conjugation of gCDs. That is favorable for the luminescence of gCDs. The intensive peak at 289.1 eV is attributed to O–C = O of carboxyl [33]. This declares the occurrence of oxidization during the hydrothermal treatment.

Summarily, IR and XP spectra suggest C = C and C = O bonds in gCDs. Alkyls, as hydrophobic groups, in gCDs are also demonstrated by the spectra, which forecast the solubility of gCDs in ethyl acetate. Nevertheless, such evidences are not adequate for explaining the mechanism of luminescence. There need more direct proofs that disclose which functional groups are responsible for the luminescence.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Demonstration of the C = C bond in gCDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 4 (a) reveals that KMnO4 destroys the fluorescence seriously. Addition of 80 μL KMnO4 depresses the intensity entirely. When gCDs solution is dropped into KMnO4 solution the pink color of KMnO4 is faded instantly and completely, which indicated in Fig. 4(b). It is certain that the C = C bonds of gCDs consume the added KMnO4 due to the intensive oxidization of KMnO4. The destruction on C = C bond results in decay of fluorescence of gCDs. Presumably, the C = C bonds should be conjugated that is responsible for the fluorescence of gCDs since the individual C = C bond can not emit fluorescence. Such facts reveal that the conjugated C = C bonds are the fluorescent pieces of gCDs.

The fluorescence of gCDs is also sensitive to NaClO solution, which exhibited in Fig. S3(a) of Supporting Information. Such solution of 40 μL leads to 40% depression of the intensity. With dropwise addition of NaClO, the PL intensity drops gradually until it disappears almost completely. This is also attributed to the oxidization of NaClO that breaks the C = C bond. Zhou and coauthors [34] attributed this to the oxidation of surface functional group and skeleton of the CDs that weakened the conjugation of the CDs. Weng [35] gave the similar assumption and considered that this was due to the oxidization of surface reductive groups of the CDs which were carbonized incompletely. They considered that NaClO destroyed the surface passivation of the CDs [36]. Herein, we provide distinct proofs which show that the intensive oxidization of KMnO4 or NaClO quenches the fluorescence of gCDs seriously. The oxidizers destroy the C = C of gCDs. The C = C bonds should be conjugated since the single C = C is not luminous. However, the signal of C = C bond is obscure in IR spectra since the stretching vibration overlaps with that of C = O. In XP spectrum, deconvolution on C 1 s signal also gives a mixed information about sp2 and sp3 C. The spectra can not provide distinct evidences of proving the C = C bonds in gCDs. Nevertheless, the approach of fluorescent quenching is an effective avenue of demonstrating the fluorophore in gCDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Demonstration of the aldehyde group of gCDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence spectrum of gCDs is also sensitive to Fehling’s solution, which displayed in Fig. 4(c). The PL intensity decreases tremendously by addition of 50 μL Fehling’s solution. They nearly quench the fluorescence completely. This implies the rapid reaction between gCDs and Fehling’s solution. Aldehyde may be a fluorophore of gCDs. The aldehyde of gCDs can also be distinguished by the reaction with Fehling’s solution. When the mixture of gCDs and Fehling’s solution is heated with hot water for several hours erythrine precipitate is produced at the bottom of the test tube, revealed in Fig. 4(d). As a matter of fact, the aldehyde may be the inheritance of glucose. Or it may be derived from intensive oxidization of glucose under hydrothermal conditions. Herein, the precipitate must not be the aldehyde of glucose since gCDs are obtained by extraction with ethyl acetate. Any inorganic impurities or intact glucose are removed completely. In fact, the individual ions of Fehling’s solution can not depress the fluorescence of gCDs solution according to our experiments. Additionally, Tollens reagent can also quench the fluorescence of gCDs which provides an evidence of demonstrating the aldehyde in gCDs too.

The oxidization of hydroxyl of glucose must be the main cause for the production of aldehyde. This is supported by hydrothermal treatment on mannitol solution. The produced fluorescent solution gives similar result, which is exhibited in Fig. S4 of Supporting Information. That witnesses the aldehyde comes from oxidization of hydroxyl truly. Likely, PL intensity of gCDs solution is sensitive to aniline (see Fig. S3(b) of Supporting Information), which also caused by interaction between the primary amino of aniline and the aldehyde of gCDs.

Summarily, the C = C bond and aldehyde of gCDs undertake the PL predominantly since the destruction for such groups causes the depression of PL intensity. This agrees with our previous conclusions [6,27]. However, the individual aldehyde can not emit fluorescence. Hence we speculate that the aldehyde and the C = C bond are conjugated in gCDs, which construct the fluorescent skeleton of gCDs. However, the lower QY as well as the shorter emission wavelength limit the practicability. In fact, the carboxyl of gCDs provides possibility of chemical modification by m-phenylenediamine that may be profitable for red shift of the fluorescent emission.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The chemical modification on gCDs with m-phenylenediamine under reflux heating
ce:section.ce:para: Though the gCDs are extracted by ethyl acetate they are still soluble in water. The surface carboxyl of gCDs suggests that they may be modified chemically by m-phenylenediamine via the amidation between carboxyl and primary amino groups. Such a modification takes place successfully under reflux heating at 80 °C for 4 h, which supported by Fig. S5(a) of Supporting Information. It reveals that the optimal excitation shifts to 400 nm with maximum emission of 516 nm. The QY of the product reaches 9.4% calculated according to the slope of the fitted line in Fig. S3(b) (the slope corresponding to RhB 6G is 7.855 × 105). This declares gCDs are reactive to m-phenylenediamine.

Such a try gives us a hint that the modification on gCDs with m-phenylenediamine may be a practical way of improving the fluorescent performance of gCDs with longer-wavelength emission. However, reflux heating may not benefit for completion of the amidation reaction.

The luminescence of gCDs is quenchable by aniline (Fig. S3(b) of Supporting Information). Herein, reflux heating on gCDs solution in the presence of m-phenylendiamine evokes an enhanced luminescence with red shift of the emission. It seems contradictory since the likeness in chemical structure for both aniline and m-phenylenediamine. Nevertheless, the fluorescence spectra indicate that gradual addition of m-phenylenediamine (2.5 mg mL−1) to the measured gCDs solution does not cause the decay of the fluorescence, which demonstrated in Fig. S6 of Supporting Information. It declares the aldehyde group in gCDs is inert to primary amino group of m-phenylenediamine in this case. We infer that the reactivity of carboxyl in gCDs is higher than that of aldehyde group for m-phenylenediamine.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hydrothermal treatment on mixture of gCDs and m-phenylenediamine
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. S5(a) suggests that the chemical modification on gCDs with m-phenylenediamine may be an efficient approach of realizing enhancement of QY of gCDs. From the view of chemical reaction kinetics, hydrothermal disposing with a high temperature and a high pressure accelerate the accomplishment of nitrogen doping. Morphology of N-gCDs is exhibited in Fig. S7 of Supporting Information. The particle sizes are greater than that of gCDs. UV–vis spectra of Fig. 5 (a) predict the reaction between gCDs and m-phenylenediamine. The n-π* transition of gCDs is 280 nm. For N-gCDs, such a transition shifts to 400 nm. An obvious red-shift predicts the newly occurred amide group and aromatic ring may involve in the π conjugation system after the chemical modification.

The red shift of n-π* transition of N-gCDs predicts red shift of the fluorescent emission, which demonstrated in Fig. 5(b). Excitation with 400 ∼ 460 nm evokes luminescence at 516 nm stably. The inset of Fig. 5(b) exhibits a bright luminescence. The emission peak in Fig. 5(b) is identical with that in Fig. S5(a) of Supporting Information. This fact suggests the similar reaction route either under hydrothermal treatment or under reflux heating, as is depicted in Fig. 1. Primary amino of m-phenylenediamine combines with carboxyl of gCDs that leads to the red shift of the emission.

The modification on gCDs is also discerned from IR spectra of Fig. 3(a) and (b) as well as Fig. 3(d). Peak 1652 cm−1 and a weak peak at 1587 cm−1 should be arisen from characteristic of amide I and amide II. The intensive peak of N-gCDs at 1615 cm−1 should be due to the overlapping of stretching vibration of C = O and C = C (v(C = O + v(C = C)). Band 3433 cm−1 belongs to v(O-H) that is intact during the modification. In Fig. 3(d), C 1 s signal of O–C = O disappears almost entirely while C = O (288.1 eV), C–O (286.0 eV), sp2–C and sp3–C (284.7 eV) remain almost unchanged compared with those of Fig. 3(c). This is due to the amidation reaction between carboxyl and primary amino of m-phenylenediamine which leads to accomplishment of nitrogen doping on gCDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of varied amounts of m-phenylenediamine on the PL intensities
ce:section.ce:para: The carboxyl groups of gCDs are reactive to amino groups of m-phenylenediamine which is a key step for implementing nitrogen doping under hydrothermal conditions. However, excess m-phenylenediamine will consume the aldehyde of gCDs inevitably, which is fundamental fluorophore according to aforementioned analyses. The consumption of aldehyde will decline the PL intensity. Fig. 5(c) confirms this speculation. The PL intensity at 516 nm varies with amounts of m-phenylenediamine. The intensity grows gradually with increment of the raw material of m-phenylenediamine from 5 to 40 mg. Amount of 60 mg m-phenylenediamine reduce the PL intensity notably. We infer that excess m-phenylenediamine consumes the aldehyde of gCDs under hydrothermal conditions that induces the drop of the PL intensity.

The QY corresponding to 5 mg m-phenylenediamine still approaches 27.0%. Amount of 25 mg m-phenylenediamine gives the highest QY of 52.7% compared with standard sample of RhB 6G, which calculated by slope of the regressed line shown in Fig. S5(c) of Supporting Information. Such performances are superior to several reported N-doped CDs. Although the dopants such as tris(hydroxymethyl) aminomethane [37], ethylenediamine [38,39], dicyandiamide [40], etc improve QY of the CDs obviously the emission are still less than 450 nm. From the view of this point, m-phenylenediamine is a wonderful nitrogen dopant for gCDs [41].

In fact, the product obtained by one-pot approach (hydrothermal treatment on mixed solutions of glucose and m-phenylenediamine) gives the same fluorescent emission, which indicated in Fig. S8(a) of Supporting Information. However, the QY of 22.0% (calculated by the slope shown in Fig. S8(b)) are much lower than that of N-gCDs.

Compared with one-pot method, the two-step method described herein provides a better understanding on the formation of N-doped CDs. The surface carboxyl groups of gCDs provide possibility of nitrogen doping. Imaginably, if the similar N-doped CDs are prepared by one-pot approach we may confused by the reaction between glucose and m-phenylenediamine since there are short of functional groups of mutual reaction. N-doped CDs prepared by urea and p-phenylenediamine [30], urea and o-phenylenediamine [42], glucose and ammonia [29], etc are the examples. Therefore, the stepwise step for preparing N-doped CDs provides an avenue of learning the approach of nitrogen doping on CDs. That is helpful for understanding the fluorescent mechanism of N-doped CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent quenching of N-gCDs by KMnO4, NaClO, aniline and Fehling’s solutions
ce:section.ce:para: Like gCDs, fluorescence of N-gCDs is still sensitive to KMnO4 or NaClO solution. Gradual additions of KMnO4 or NaClO to the measured N-gCDs solution cause quenching of the fluorescence, which exhibited in Fig. 6 (a) and (b) clearly. This also attributes to the disruption of the C = C bonds in N-gCDs, which is a vital pieces for the luminescence. That agrees with the analyses on gCDs. This is also witnessed by Fig. 7 (a ∼ c). Color of KMnO4 solution is faded completely by addition of N-gCDs solution. When N-gCDs solution is mixed NaClO firstly, the added KMnO4 solution does not fade again. It indicates the C = C bonds in N-gCDs have been destroyed by NaClO. The color of successively added KMnO4 solution does not change any more. The modification with m-phenylenediamine herein does not alter the conjugated skeleton of C = C bonds.

Fig. 6(c) and the inset indicate that 1 μL Fehling’s solution quench the fluorescence of N-gCDs completely. This is similar to the fluorescent response of gCDs for Fehling’s solution. It implies that the aldehyde groups are still involved in the core of luminescence. However, PL intensity of N-gCDs remains stable with addition of Cu2+ shown in Fig. S9 of Supporting Information.

The fluorescence can also be quenched by aniline solution shown in Fig. 6(d). This is in accord with the fact that the excess m-phenylenediamine destroys the fluorescence exhibited in Fig. 5(c). In fact, PL intensity of the measured N-gCDs solution can also be reduced by addition of m-phenylenediamine, which exhibited in Fig. S10 of Supporting Information.

Fig. 7(d) and (e) also provide the evidences of proving the aldehyde in N-gCDs. The erythrine precipitate is deposited at bottom of the test tube shown in Fig. 7(d), which resulted by reducibility of aldehyde in N-gCDs. When N-gCDs solution is mixed with aniline firstly the Cu2+ in the added Fehling’s solution can not be reduced then since the aldehyde has been consumed by aniline in the first place. Hence there is no precipitate any more, which is proved by Fig. 7(e).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analyses on some geometrical parameters of the fluorescent models of gCDs and N-gCDs
ce:section.ce:para: We have demonstrated that the C = C bonds and the aldehyde are the fundamental fluorophore of gCDs or N-gCDs. Numerous organics illustrate individual C = C bond or aldehyde can not lead to fluorescence. The C = C bonds and the aldehyde should be conjugated and construct a rigid skeleton that undertake the fluorescence. Also, the carboxyl of gCDs should be attached directly to the fluorophore of gCDs since the chemical modification with m-phenylenediamine changes the fluorescent emission. Hydroxyl and methylene should also be included in the models, as is demonstrated by IR and XP spectra measurements. According to these analyses, the fluorescent skeletons of gCDs and N-gCDs are constructed, which exhibited in Fig. 8 (a) and (b) (represented with Model1 Model2 respectively). Ab initio calculation on Model1 shows the fluorescent emission of 446.54 nm which is comparable to the practical fluorescence measurement (453 nm in Fig. 2(b)). This suggests the rationality of Model1. The calculation on Model2 shows the fluorescent emission of 498.53 nm which almost approaches practical measurement (516 nm of Fig. 6(a)). The part dihedrals of the models after geometrical optimization are summarized in Table 1 .

Table 1 exhibit that the fluorescent skeleton of Model1, constructed by C1, C2, C3, C4, C5, C6, C7 and O10 as well as the carboxyl, are almost coplanar. Chemical modification with m-phenylenediamine disturbs the fluorescent skeleton slightly judged from dihedrals of C1-C2-C3-C4, C3-C4-C5-C6 and C5-C6-C7-O10. These dihedrals in Model2 derivate more from the coplane compared with those of Model1. However, the amide and the benzene ring are almost coplanar since the dihedrals of C8-O9-N11-H12 and H12-N11-C13-C14 are 3.0° and 0.5° respectively. They are partly coplanar with the fluorescent skeleton judged from dihedral of C4-C3-C8-O9 of 34.1°. The red-shift emission of N-gCDs should be due to the part conjugation among the amide, benzene ring and the fluorescent skeleton of gCDs. The amide and benzene involve in the conjugation that enlarges the coplane and decrease the energy difference between HOMO and LUMO from Model1 of 2.7766 eV to Model2 of 2.4870 eV. The amide herein acts as a bridge of connecting the coplanar benzene ring and the fluorescent skeleton of gCDs. This may be the reason for the red-shift emission when aromatic amines are involved in the preparation of N-doped CDs [30,42,43]. Conversely, aliphatic amines [17,19] can not realize red shift of emission.

Calculations with the same method and the same basis set are carried out on the models obtained by chemical modifications on gCDs with o-phenylenediamine or p-phenylenediamine, which indicate in Fig. S11 of Supporting Information. The fluorescent emissions of such models are 417.30 nm and 530.83 nm respectively. That declares the modification by o-phenylenediamine leads to blue shift of the emission while the one modified by p-phenylenediamine results in red shift. These agree well with the fluorescent measurements corresponding to 428 and 521 nm respectively, which indicated in Fig. S12 of Supporting Information. Such theoretical emissions accord with practical measurements qualitatively, which suggests the rationality of the fluorescent models.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cellular imaging in the present of N-gCDs
ce:section.ce:para: MTT method reveals the lower toxicity of N-gCDs. Cellular viability of MCF-7 exceeds 95% in N-gCDs solution of 0.05 mg mL−1, revealed by histogram of Fig. S13 of Supporting Information. The higher QY of N-gCDs ensures an excellent performance of cellular imaging. Fig. 9 indicates the fluorescent N-gCDs can penetrate cytomembrane of MCF-7 and stain cytoplasm and nucleus effectively. Most of them are localized in regions of cytoplasm and nucleus. Such a performance is superior to several reported N-doped CDs. For example, bis(3-aminopropyl)-terminated oligomeric poly(ethyleneglycol) or 2,2′-(ethylenedioxy)bis(ethylamine) passivated CDs are accumulated in cytomembrane of MCF-7 during the cellar cultivation [44]. The N-doped CDs prepared by o-, m- or p-phenylenediamine can not pierce cytomembrane of MCF-7 effectively [45]. The CDs prepared with histidine give a less bright cellular imaging (AD-293 cells) due to lower QY [11]. The CDs obtained by chitosan can penetrate into the cell but do not enter nucleolus even though they have a high QY [3].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conclusions
ce:section.ce:para: We provide experimental evidences to demonstrate the fluorophore in gCDs. Purification on gCDs ensures the accuracy of the research. Moreover, we disclose the approach of nitrogen doping on gCDs and realize red shift of the emission with remarkable enhancement of QY. The carboxyl on gCDs provides change of the modification by appropriate quantity of m-phenylenediamine that enlarges the conjugation area and decreases the energy gap between HOMO and LUMO of N-gCDs. That is the key for the red shift of the emission of N-gCDs. The red question mark in Fig. 1 should be attributed to the conjugated C = C bond or aldehyde since a slight disturbing on them causes notable drop of PL intensity of gCDs or N-gCDs. Such conclusions are beneficial for understanding of the approach of nitrogen doping and the fluorescent mechanism of N-doped CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CRediT authorship contribution statement
ce:section.ce:para: Jian Yang: Investigation, Writing - original draft. Yuen Muk Fung: Investigation. Peng Wu: Investigation. Yuan Li: Investigation. Xiang Liu: Supervision, Methodology. Kui Zhang: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by grants from Shenzhen Fundamental Research Fund (JCYJ20190813172609404).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Supplementary data
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2023.115197.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Supplementary data

Sensor. Actuat. B-Chem.
